title: Slow prompt return when starting user quadlet container & repeated `sh: inactive` output in journalctl.
body:
I don't think this is an _issue_ (except perhaps the journal log spam without any attached details) with podman or quadlet, but the behaviour might catch some people out so I am listing this here for search purposes.

I wasn't sure at this time how a potential documentation PR would read without getting into the guts of describing systemd behaviour and system specific conditions, which felt like it starts to wander off topic.

```
podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.3.0
API Version:  5.3.0
Go Version:   go1.23.3
Git Commit:   874bf2c301ecf0ba645f1bb45f81966cc755b7da
Built:        Sun Nov 17 02:31:45 2024
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

By default user quadlets depend on `podman-user-wait-network-online` which simply checks if `systemctl is-active network-online.target` returns `active`. 

If your system does not have any services that depend on `network-online.target` during boot, then `systemctl is-active network-online.target` will always return `inactive`. This value is not updated after the system has booted.

The container will still start, but you will wait 90 seconds for your prompt to return and also see lots of `<host> sh[<pid>]: inactive` entries in journalctl.

As the documentation covers, you can put the following in your container file to remove the default dependency on `podman-user-wait-network-online`.

```ini
[Quadlet]
DefaultDependencies=false
```

Alternatively, you may create a simple unit file that depends on `network-online.target` and enable it. This will pull in `network-online.target` during boot and subsequent invocations of `systemctl is-active network-online.target` will return `active`.

```ini
# /etc/systemd/system/wait-for-network.service

[Unit]
Description=Wait for Network Online
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/true

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

You may also manually run `systemctl start network-online.target` with the appropriate permissions.

Note: `network-online.target` is **not** intended for "is the network currently still up?" checks, only during boot, so removing this dependency as the documentation describes should have little practical impact for manually started user-quadlets, where the user should already know the link status.

Note: waiting for `network-online.target` will add *some* time to your boot -- the time for the link to configure and its state to change to `UP`.

Note: `network-online.target` does not check any routing conditions (eg: "internet access"), only the link state.
